A History of Mind
Home
Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Reference

Glossary

Working definitions for the vocabulary of contemporary philosophy of mind.

A B C D E F G H I J M N O P Q R S T V W

A

Access consciousness

Access consciousness refers to mental content that is available for reasoning, verbal report, deliberate action, and flexible control. The term is often contrasted with phenomenal consciousness: a state might play a broad cognitive role in guiding thought and behavior even if philosophers still debate whether that fully captures its felt character.

Attention schema

Attention schema theory says the brain builds a simplified internal model of its own attention, much as it builds models of bodies and objects in the world. On this view, consciousness talk grows partly out of the system’s representation of what it is attending to and how that attention is organized.

Abduction / inference to the best explanation

A form of reasoning in which we compare competing explanations and favor the one that best accounts for the evidence with the fewest ad hoc assumptions. It is weaker than strict deduction, but central in both science and philosophy when we are choosing among large-scale theories. This is one of the main methodological themes: naturalist/materialist views are favored not because alternatives are logically impossible, but because they explain the evidence better.

Agency

Agency is the capacity of a system to act in ways that are organized around its own maintenance, goals, or adaptive responses rather than merely being passively pushed around. Agency is treated as gradual rather than all-or-nothing: even very simple organisms can show primitive agency when they detect aspects of their environment and move or regulate themselves accordingly, long before anything like reflective human consciousness appears.

Allothetic appearance

An allothetic appearance is an appearance that points beyond itself to something else: a cause, source, referent, object, event, or underlying state of affairs. This is the central notion for understanding why images are not true or false in isolation. Their truth depends on whether a subject can correctly establish the connection between the appearance and what it is an appearance of. AI becomes philosophically disruptive, on this view, not because it creates appearances ex nihilo, but because it can sever or obscure those connections.

Anamorphosis

Anamorphosis is a term for the appearance of a new form or level through reorganization and restructuring rather than creation out of nothing. In the transcript it is discussed as a rival to the language of emergence, but also as a more limited concept, because the speakers argue that contemporary talk of emergence better highlights the roles of process, mechanism, and environmental interaction in producing qualitative novelty.

Anthropomorphism

Anthropomorphism is the tendency to attribute human-like mental states, motives, emotions, or intentions to non-human entities such as animals, machines, or natural systems. In discussions of AI, it matters because fluent language and familiar social cues can easily make systems seem more understanding, caring, or self-aware than the evidence warrants. The interview repeatedly warns against projecting suffering, empathy, or fully human-style inner life onto machines too quickly.

Active inference

Active inference is the idea, associated especially with Karl Friston and allied predictive-processing work, that organisms do not merely predict the world but also act so as to reduce prediction error. Perception and action are therefore tightly linked: a system maintains itself by updating its models and by changing the world or its own bodily state to bring sensory input into line with its expectations.

Artificial neural network

An artificial neural network is a layered computational system made of many simple processing nodes connected by weighted pathways. During training, successful pathways are reinforced and unsuccessful ones are weakened, allowing the system to improve at tasks such as pattern recognition, classification, and some forms of inference from incomplete data. Stresses that these systems are only loosely inspired by biological brains: they can learn, but they are not miniature versions of neural tissue.

Autotic appearance

An autotic appearance is an appearance taken as self-contained or self-explanatory, one that does not need to be referred to anything beyond itself in order to count as what it is. The transcript uses this contrast to explain deception: trouble begins when an appearance that is really allothetic is interpreted as if it were autotic. In the AI case, an image may present itself with the force of immediate presence even when its real source, construction, or referent remains inaccessible.

B

Behaviorism

Behaviorism is a broad approach in psychology that explains behavior in terms of observable action, environmental context, learning history, and functional relations rather than by appealing first to hidden mental entities. Its stronger forms are often caricatured as denying inner life altogether, but in practice behaviorists have often tried instead to redescribe mental vocabulary in empirically tractable terms. Behaviorism supplies the main framework for discussing thought, consciousness, language, and AI without reifying them into mysterious inner substances.

Biological naturalism

Biological naturalism is John Searle’s view that consciousness is a real higher-level feature of biological organisms, caused by and realized in brain processes without being reducible to mere syntax or abstract computation alone. The position rejects both substance dualism and the idea that consciousness is an illusion, while insisting that the right kind of living biological organization matters to mindedness.

C

Category mistake / category error

A category mistake occurs when something is treated as belonging to the wrong logical or conceptual kind—for example, speaking as if an abstraction were a separate thing or substance. Gilbert Ryle famously used the idea to criticize ways of talking about the mind that wrongly make it sound like an additional object inside the body. The charge is that talking about “consciousness” as if it were an independently existing entity can mislead us in exactly this way.

Cartesian theater

The Cartesian theater is Daniel Dennett’s label for the tempting but misleading picture according to which consciousness happens in a single privileged inner place where “it all comes together” for an inner observer. The phrase is used critically: it names a bad model of the mind, one that reintroduces a homunculus or central witness precisely where explanation is needed.

Causal closure of the physical

The idea that physical events have sufficient physical causes. In philosophy of mind, this matters because if brain events are already explained within the physical domain, then a non-physical soul or mind would have no clear causal role unless it somehow interfered with physical processes. The speakers use closure and conservation-style reasoning as one of their main objections to dualism.

Cognitive architecture

A cognitive architecture is the overall organization of a mind-like system: the set of components, bottlenecks, memory systems, control loops, and information pathways that structure how it works. Philosophers and cognitive scientists use the term when they want to explain mentality at the level of system design rather than at the level of single neurons or isolated behaviors.

Collective intentionality

Collective intentionality is the shared aboutness or directedness involved in genuinely joint activity: intending, believing, deciding, or committing as a “we” rather than merely as many separate individuals in parallel. It matters for social philosophy of mind because some forms of thinking and agency seem to exist only in coordinated groups, institutions, or practices.

Chemotaxis

Chemotaxis is the directed movement of an organism or cell in response to chemical gradients, such as moving toward nutrients or away from harmful substances. In the video it functions as a key example of primitive biological agency: bacteria do not consciously deliberate, but they do differentially detect and respond to features of their environment in ways that matter for survival.

Computationism

In philosophy of mind, computationism is the view that mental activity is fundamentally a kind of computation, often expressed by analogy with software running on hardware. Mentions it as one of the major positions in the field, but his treatment is skeptical: he thinks the analogy captures something about information processing while obscuring the biological, embodied, and dynamic character of actual brains. In that sense, the term matters mainly as a contrast case for his more materialist and organism-centered view.

Connectome

A connectome is a map of neural connections in a brain or nervous system. More loosely, it refers to the organized network structure through which different brain regions communicate. The term appears in discussion of how mental life is related to brain organization and why brain-based explanations are more fruitful than appeals to an immaterial soul.

Consciousness

Usually, consciousness means subjective awareness: there is something it is like to have an experience, feel pain, see a color, think a thought, or be aware of oneself or the world. The interview treats consciousness as fully real, but rejects the idea that it must therefore belong to a separate, supernatural realm. A major point of the conversation is that consciousness is difficult, but not uniquely exempt from natural explanation.

Corporeal operative subject

The corporeal operative subject is the embodied acting subject: not a disembodied mind, but a living body that perceives, moves, manipulates, investigates, and establishes relations through concrete operations. In the discussion, this notion matters because appearance and truth are inseparable from the work of subjects who look, compare, touch, test, and connect phenomena to their sources. It also anchors the critique of strong AI anthropomorphism: a machine that processes symbols is not thereby a human-style operative subject.

D

Deep learning

Deep learning is a family of machine-learning techniques that use multilayer artificial neural networks to extract patterns from large amounts of data and solve tasks that simpler algorithms handle poorly. The additional layers allow many possible internal pathways, and training gradually adjusts their weights so the system becomes better at recognition, prediction, or classification. Deep learning is presented as one of the main reasons current AI can handle image recognition and other inverse-problem tasks that older fixed-rule systems struggled with.

Direct problem

A direct problem is a problem in which the relevant input data are already known and the task is to derive the corresponding output from them. Standard algorithms excel at direct problems because, given the same input, they follow a determinate sequence of steps and return the same result. Uses the notion to contrast ordinary computation with the harder task of inferring hidden causes or structures from partial evidence.

Downward causation

Downward causation is the idea that higher-level organized systems can constrain, regulate, or influence the behavior of their lower-level components. The speakers use the term to reject the claim that emergent properties must be causally inert: wholes do not float free of their parts, but neither are they explanatorily empty, since organized systems shape what their parts can do within a given structure.

Dualism

In philosophy of mind, dualism is the view that mind and body, or the mental and the physical, are fundamentally different kinds of thing. Some forms say mental phenomena are non-physical; others say the mind is not reducible to the brain. The interview treats dualism as a major target of criticism, especially where it turns consciousness into a separate ontological domain.

E

Embodied cognition

Embodied cognition is the view that thinking does not happen in a sealed inner chamber detached from bodily life. Perception, movement, posture, sensation, action skills, and environmental interaction help constitute cognition itself rather than merely supplying inputs to an otherwise self-contained intellect.

Extended mind

The extended mind thesis says that, under the right conditions, parts of the environment can function as genuine components of a cognitive process rather than as mere external aids. A notebook, interface, tool, or digital device can count as part of thinking when it is reliably integrated into the agent’s ongoing cognitive economy in the right way.

Eliminativism / eliminative materialism

A radical materialist view according to which some common-sense ways of talking about the mind are so misleading that mature science may eventually discard them rather than simply refine them. Eliminativists often focus on “folk psychology” categories such as belief, desire, or certain ordinary ways of talking about consciousness. The speakers distance themselves from hard-line eliminativism: they do not deny experience, but they do reject reified talk of soul, self, or mind as separate entities.

Emergence

A general idea according to which higher-level properties or patterns arise from lower-level processes when they are organized in sufficiently complex ways. Emergence is often invoked to explain why life, mind, or consciousness can be genuinely novel without being supernatural. It is used to argue that consciousness can be a real qualitative novelty without being an ontological rupture.

Emergentism

A philosophical position that applies the idea of emergence specifically to cases like life or mind. In philosophy of mind, emergentists typically claim that consciousness arises from physical systems while still being a distinctive higher-level phenomenon. The interview is broadly sympathetic to a naturalized, non-miraculous form of emergentism, though the speakers resist vague or mystical uses of the term.

Epiphenomenalism

The view that conscious experiences exist but do not themselves causally affect physical events. On this picture, brain activity produces conscious experience, but consciousness does not do any causal work in behavior. The speakers reject this sort of picture: they do not treat consciousness as an inert byproduct, even though they emphasize that conscious processes depend on broader bodily and neural processes.

Eukaryotic revolution / symbiogenesis

The eukaryotic revolution refers to the evolutionary transition from simpler prokaryotic life to eukaryotic cells, which have nuclei and organelles such as mitochondria. In the transcript this transition is linked to symbiogenesis, associated with Lynn Margulis: the idea that major biological novelties can arise through long-term symbiotic incorporation of once-separate organisms. It matters here because it is presented as a concrete evolutionary case of emergence through organization, integration, and new levels of complexity.

Evil genius

The evil genius is Descartes’ skeptical hypothesis of a powerful deceiver capable of misleading us even about what seem to be the most certain truths. The figure is reconsidered less as a timeless argument than as a comparison point for AI-enabled deception. The speaker’s key claim is that current AI does not matter philosophically because it creates a rival world, but because it can function like a practical evil genius by disconnecting appearances from identifiable referents.

F

Formal television

In this framework, formal television is live television: a transmission of images being produced in real time rather than a finished, fixed audiovisual work like a film. The transcript treats this idea as crucial because AI-generated insertions create a new problem precisely inside live broadcast, where viewers ordinarily take the image to remain connected to a present event. The issue is not simply falsity in general, but the corruption of that live connection.

Free will

Very broadly, free will is the kind of control over one’s actions that might be thought necessary for responsibility, choice, or agency. Philosophers disagree about whether free will is compatible with determinism, whether it requires indeterminism, or whether the ordinary concept is confused. Free will only appears near the end, but it is treated in continuity with the broader theme that human agency should be understood naturalistically rather than through metaphysical mythmaking.

Functionalism

In philosophy of mind, functionalism holds that what makes something a mental state is not the material it is made of by itself, but the causal or functional role it plays within a larger system. A state counts as pain, belief, perception, or memory partly because of how it relates to inputs, outputs, and other internal states. In the AI interview, functionalism is treated sympathetically but only in a disciplined form: the speaker accepts attention to functions and capacities, while rejecting the idea that mentality could be realized in absolutely any substrate whatsoever.

G

Global workspace theory

Global Workspace Theory holds that a mental content becomes conscious when it wins access to a limited-capacity “workspace” from which it can be broadcast to many specialized processes at once. The theory is meant to explain why conscious contents are especially useful for report, deliberation, learning, and flexible control in a distributed cognitive system.

Global neuronal workspace

Global Neuronal Workspace is the neuroscientific development of global-workspace ideas, associated especially with Stanislas Dehaene, Jean-Pierre Changeux, and collaborators. It proposes that consciousness involves large-scale ignition and broadcasting across distributed neural networks, especially when information becomes globally available for report, reasoning, and control.

H

Higher-order theory

Higher-order theories say a mental state becomes conscious when the subject is, in some suitable way, aware of being in that state. Different versions appeal to higher-order thoughts, perceptions, or representations, but they share the idea that consciousness involves a mental state being taken up by another level of awareness.

Hard problem of consciousness

A term associated with David Chalmers. It is the problem of explaining why physical or functional processes are accompanied by subjective experience at all—why there is “something it is like” to be a conscious organism. The speakers treat the hard problem in its dramatic formulation as something that risks becoming a pseudo-problem unless it is broken down into more concrete empirical and philosophical questions.

I

Idealism

A family of philosophical views according to which reality is fundamentally mental, mind-dependent, or in some deep sense constituted by consciousness or ideas. In philosophy of mind, idealism opposes materialist attempts to explain mind in physical terms. The speakers mention idealism mainly as an alternative they reject, or at least see as less explanatorily fruitful than naturalism/materialism.

Identity theory

Identity theory says that mental states are not merely caused by brain states or correlated with them; they are brain states. In other words, the mental and the neural are not two separate things standing in a causal relation, but two descriptions of the same underlying process. This is the preferred view in the AI interview, where Óscar Teixidó presents it as the strongest anti-dualist framework.

Interactionist dualism

Interactionist dualism is the form of dualism according to which mind and body are distinct, yet can causally affect one another. On this view, a mental decision or volition can alter bodily behavior, while bodily states can also inform or affect the mind. This is treated as the classical soul-body picture, and one of his main objections is that it offers no intelligible mechanism for how an immaterial thing could transfer energy or exert causal influence on a material organism.

Integrated information theory

Integrated Information Theory proposes that consciousness corresponds to the degree and structure of integrated information generated by a system. Very roughly, the theory claims that a conscious state is one whose causal organization is both highly unified and highly informative, though the view remains controversial because its mathematical ambitions and metaphysical implications are heavily debated.

Integrated information

Integrated information is the notion that a system can generate unified causal structure that is not reducible to a mere collection of separate parts operating independently. In consciousness science the term matters because Integrated Information Theory treats the quantity and organization of such integration as central to explaining why some systems may support conscious experience.

Introspection

The attempt to know one’s own mental life by attending inwardly to one’s experiences, thoughts, feelings, or sensations. Introspection can provide real information, but philosophers and psychologists have long argued that it is also fallible, theory-laden, and often misleading. This is central to the interview: one of the speakers’ strongest points is that people trust introspection too much and mistake it for privileged access to the true structure of mind.

Intentionality

Intentionality is the “aboutness” or directedness of mental life: thoughts are about things, perceptions present objects, desires aim at outcomes, and beliefs represent the world as being a certain way. It is one of the central technical terms in philosophy of mind because it marks the difference between a mere physical event and a state with content or reference.

Inverse problem

An inverse problem is a problem in which we start from an observed effect, trace, signal, or incomplete body of evidence and try to reconstruct the cause or structure that produced it. Such problems are typically underdetermined, because many different causes may fit the same observable result. Treats inverse problems as central both to ordinary life and to science, and he uses them to explain why modern AI matters: learning systems can sometimes be trained to handle this kind of uncertainty better than simple fixed algorithms.

J

Joint attention

Joint attention is the coordinated focus of two or more subjects on the same object, event, or situation, together with some awareness of that shared focus. It is a foundational concept in developmental psychology and social cognition because it helps explain how language learning, teaching, cooperation, and shared understanding get off the ground.

M

Material television

Material television refers to television or screen media considered as finished content, recorded sequences, edited products, or audiovisual material treated more like cinema than like live transmission. This category helps mark the limit of the problem: manipulated or fictional images in film, YouTube, or other non-live media are not philosophically equivalent to AI intrusions into formal television. The latter are more troubling because they exploit the evidential force of live appearance.

Materialism

In this context, materialism is the view that mental phenomena depend on material or physical processes rather than on an immaterial soul. Contemporary materialism in philosophy of mind is often broader than crude reductionism: it can allow for complexity, embodiment, organization, and emergence while still denying a separate mental substance. One recurring point in the interview is that materialism is often caricatured, both philosophically and morally, and that this caricature should be resisted.

Mental representation

A mental representation is a state that carries content by standing in for, depicting, modeling, or presenting something else. Philosophers disagree about exactly how representation works, but the term is used for beliefs, perceptions, images, concepts, maps, and other states that let a system be guided by what something means rather than only by its immediate physical surroundings.

Mental causation

Mental causation is the problem of how thoughts, intentions, beliefs, pains, and other mental states can make a real causal difference in a world seemingly governed by physical processes. It is one of the classic pressure points in philosophy of mind because dualism, physicalism, functionalism, and epiphenomenalism all handle the causal role of the mental in sharply different ways.

Metacognition

Metacognition is cognition about cognition: monitoring, evaluating, or regulating one’s own perception, memory, reasoning, confidence, or problem-solving. The term matters in philosophy of mind and cognitive science because it helps distinguish merely having a mental state from being able to reflect on, report on, or strategically control that state.

Mindreading

Mindreading is the capacity to interpret the behavior of others by attributing mental states such as beliefs, desires, intentions, perceptions, or emotions to them. The term overlaps with theory of mind, though it often emphasizes the practical, interpretive activity of understanding another agent rather than the possession of a general theoretical capacity.

Multiple drafts model

The Multiple Drafts Model is Daniel Dennett’s theory that consciousness does not arise at one privileged inner point where everything becomes present to a central observer. Instead, multiple parallel processes unfold across the brain, and what counts as conscious depends on how some contents get stabilized, used, and made available within those distributed processes.

Meta-problem of consciousness

A term used especially in recent philosophy of mind. Instead of asking directly what consciousness is, it asks why consciousness seems so puzzling to us, why we talk about it the way we do, and why we are inclined to treat it as uniquely mysterious. The interview gives this issue major importance: the speakers suggest that part of the “mystery” of consciousness comes from cognitive habits, introspective overconfidence, and inherited cultural assumptions.

Moral status

Moral status refers to the degree to which an entity matters morally for its own sake, rather than merely instrumentally. Beings with moral status may be owed consideration, protection, or rights, even if they do not have the same responsibilities or capacities as adult humans. In the AI interview, this becomes important when asking whether a genuinely conscious AI would deserve rights, legal recognition, or protections analogous to those discussed for non-human animals.

Multiple realizability

This is the claim that the same higher-level property or function can be realized by different lower-level physical configurations. In philosophy of mind, it is often used to argue that mentality need not depend on one exact microphysical layout. In the AI interview, multiple realizability is accepted in a limited way: it supports openness to non-biological consciousness in principle, but not the claim that any material arrangement at all could generate mind.

N

Naturalism

The view that reality belongs to the natural world and should be understood in continuity with the sciences rather than by appeal to supernatural beings or non-natural substances. In philosophy of mind, naturalism means that mind and consciousness must be explained as part of nature, not as exceptions to it. This is one of the interview’s master terms, and the speakers repeatedly frame their position as a scientifically informed naturalism.

Neutral monism

Neutral monism is the view that the fundamental stuff of reality is neither mental nor physical in itself, but something more basic from which both the mental and the physical can be derived or organized. Philosophers such as William James, Bertrand Russell, and some contemporary theorists have used it to avoid both dualism and reductive materialism.

Nonreductive physicalism

Nonreductive physicalism is the view that everything that exists is physical or physically grounded, while still holding that higher-level properties such as mental states cannot be straightforwardly reduced to lower-level physical descriptions. It tries to preserve both physicalism and the explanatory importance of psychological, biological, or social levels of analysis.

Neutral appearance

A neutral appearance is an appearance that is not functioning straightforwardly as either a truthful appearance or a deceptive one. The transcript uses the idea for cases like theatrical representation, where something appears as something else without necessarily misleading us. AI-generated imagery pushes this category to its limit, because some appearances may have to be treated provisionally as neutral when their connection to any determinate referent cannot be securely established.

O

Ontological novelty / qualitative novelty

Ontological novelty is the appearance of genuinely new ways of being in reality; qualitative novelty is the more specific claim that a system can have properties its constituent parts do not have on their own. This is one of the transcript’s central ideas. The speakers argue that such novelty is real without requiring miracle or creation ex nihilo: new properties arise from composition, structure, process, mechanism, and environmental relation.

Operant conditioning

Operant conditioning is a form of learning in which behavior is shaped by its consequences, such as reinforcement, punishment, or other feedback contingencies. Responses that reliably produce successful outcomes become more likely, while unsuccessful ones are weakened or revised. This idea is used to interpret both human problem solving and some AI training-and-feedback loops, especially where systems generate outputs, receive results, and then adjust future behavior.

P

Panpsychism

The view that mentality or consciousness is fundamental and widespread throughout nature, perhaps present in some form even at the most basic levels of reality. Panpsychism is often presented as a way of avoiding both strict materialism and substance dualism. It appears as one of the rival positions the speakers mention but do not think has the same explanatory power as a more standard naturalist/materialist approach.

Phenomenal consciousness

Phenomenal consciousness is conscious experience considered in its felt or first-person aspect: what it is like to see red, taste bitterness, feel pain, or undergo joy. The term is often used to mark the part of consciousness that seems hardest to explain in purely functional or behavioral terms.

Predictive processing

Predictive processing is the family of theories according to which the brain constantly generates predictions about incoming signals and updates itself by reducing prediction error. On this picture, perception is not passive reception but active model-guided inference shaped by the body, the environment, and ongoing attempts to keep surprise manageable.

Personal identity

The philosophical problem of what makes a person the same person over time. Competing theories appeal to memory, psychology, bodily continuity, narrative continuity, or other criteria. Personal identity is treated as much less stable or substantial than common sense often assumes; the self is presented more as a shifting organization of processes than as an eternal core.

Physicalism

A close relative of materialism and often used almost interchangeably with it in contemporary analytic philosophy. Physicalism says that everything is physical, or that everything ultimately depends on what physics and the special sciences describe. The interview sometimes uses “materialism” in places where many philosophers today would more specifically say “physicalism,” especially when discussing strong anti-dualist positions.

Private speech / inner speech

Private speech or inner speech is language directed at oneself rather than at another person. In developmental and behaviorist accounts, overt social speech can gradually become abbreviated, internalized, and used for self-guidance, planning, rehearsal, and problem solving. This idea is central to the claim that thinking is, in an important sense, socially acquired language turned inward.

Problem of indiscernibles

Here the problem of indiscernibles is the practical and epistemic difficulty of distinguishing a generated appearance from an ordinary recorded or directly perceived one when no reliable visual criterion remains available. The transcript treats this as one of AI’s central philosophical effects. Once artificial images become indistinguishable from non-artificial ones, the ordinary route from appearance to truth is destabilized, especially in contexts like law, journalism, and live broadcasting.

Property dualism

Property dualism is the view that there is only one kind of substance, but that mental properties are nevertheless irreducible to physical properties. Unlike substance dualism, it does not posit an immaterial soul as a separate thing; instead, it claims that consciousness or experience introduces a distinct category of properties within an otherwise physical world. Mentions this position in passing when noting that some contemporary philosophers resist the label “dualist” while still treating the mental as fundamentally non-physical in an important sense.

Psychophysical parallelism

Psychophysical parallelism is the view that mental events and physical events develop in strict coordination without directly interacting. The mind and the body run on parallel tracks, matching one another point by point, but neither causes the other. Presents it as a historically important dualist strategy for avoiding the interaction problem, while also implying that it solves little, since the apparent harmony between mind and body is left unexplained except by an implausibly prearranged order.

Q

Qualia

A term philosophers often use for the felt, subjective, phenomenal aspects of experience—for example, what pain feels like, or what it is like to see red. Many debates in philosophy of mind concern whether qualia can be fully explained in physical, functional, or representational terms. Subjective experience is taken seriously, but the speakers resist treating qualia as evidence for a second substance or a metaphysical soul.

R

Representationalism

Representationalism is the view that at least much of mentality—and often consciousness in particular—can be explained in terms of representational content. Roughly, what a mental state is and what it feels like depend importantly on how it represents the world, the body, or the subject’s situation.

Recurrent processing theory

Recurrent Processing Theory holds that consciousness depends not merely on signals moving forward through a perceptual system, but on recurrent or feedback loops in which activity is sent back and integrated across levels. The theory is often presented as an alternative to global-workspace style views, emphasizing local recurrent dynamics rather than full-system broadcasting.

Semantic externalism

Semantic externalism is the view that the content of at least some thoughts depends partly on factors outside the individual’s head, such as social-linguistic practice, environmental relations, or causal history. It matters for philosophy of mind because it challenges the assumption that mental content is fixed entirely by what is internally present in the subject.

S

Sentience

Sentience is the capacity to have felt experiences, especially experiences with a positive or negative valence such as pleasure, pain, suffering, or enjoyment. It is often treated as one of the most morally significant features a being can have, because it marks the capacity to be harmed or benefited in an experiential sense. In the AI interview, sentience is closely tied to the question of whether an artificial system could count as a serious moral subject.

Self-consciousness

Self-consciousness is awareness of oneself as oneself: not just having experiences, but in some sense taking oneself as the subject of thought, perception, memory, action, or reflection. Philosophers use the term in several ways, ranging from minimal self-awareness to reflective self-knowledge, so the exact notion depends on context.

Social cognition

Social cognition is the set of capacities by which subjects perceive, interpret, predict, and coordinate with other agents: recognizing intentions, emotions, norms, attention, roles, and shared situations. The term is useful whenever philosophy of mind moves beyond isolated individuals to communication, imitation, cooperation, and social understanding.

Shared intentionality

Shared intentionality is the capacity of multiple agents to participate in genuinely shared goals, plans, attention, or commitments rather than merely acting side by side. It is especially important in work by Michael Tomasello and related developmental theorists who argue that many distinctively human forms of cognition and culture depend on this robustly cooperative structure.

Self-model

A self-model is an internal representation or organizational structure by which a system tracks itself as a body, agent, perspective, or continuing subject. Different theories use the term differently, but it is often invoked to explain self-awareness, bodily ownership, perspective-taking, and the sense that experiences are happening to me.

Supervenience

Supervenience is a dependence relation according to which there can be no difference at one level without some difference at another base level. In philosophy of mind, the standard thought is that there can be no mental difference without some physical difference, even if that does not by itself tell us whether mental properties are reducible to physical ones.

Strong emergence

Strong emergence usually means a form of emergence so autonomous from lower-level structure and process that it cannot in principle be explained, derived, or integrated into an ordinary naturalistic account. The speakers treat this notion skeptically. They argue that the term is often defined inconsistently, mixes epistemic and ontological issues, and should not be used to place consciousness outside the ordinary dependence of higher-level phenomena on material organization.

Subjectivity / private world

Subjectivity refers to first-person experience as lived from within: what it is like for a subject to perceive, feel, think, or undergo something. Calling it a “private world” emphasizes that, unlike publicly observable behavior, it is directly accessible only to the subject and must be inferred in others. The interview leans heavily on this notion when arguing that neither human nor machine subjectivity can be read off with certainty from behavior alone, even though behavior remains our main evidence.

Substance dualism

The classical Cartesian form of dualism, according to which mind and body are two different substances: the body is physical, while the mind or soul is immaterial. The central problem for this view is explaining how two radically different substances could interact. The interview repeatedly criticizes this kind of soul-based picture as both conceptually weak and scientifically unmotivated.

Substrate

A substrate is the underlying material basis in which a process occurs. In philosophy of mind and AI, the substrate question asks whether consciousness requires very specific kinds of physical organization, such as biological brains, or whether it could also arise in other sufficiently similar physical systems. The AI interview repeatedly stresses that consciousness may not be biologically exclusive while still requiring a narrow range of suitable substrates.

Suffering

Suffering is negatively valenced conscious experience that involves distress, pain, anguish, frustration, or some other form of felt badness. It is morally important because a being that can genuinely suffer can be harmed in a direct experiential sense, not just damaged instrumentally. Suffering functions as the key threshold question for AI ethics: not merely whether a system is intelligent, but whether it could ever have experiences that make moral concern urgently appropriate.

T

Theory of mind

Theory of mind is the capacity to attribute perceptions, beliefs, intentions, emotions, and other mental states to other agents and to use those attributions to interpret or predict behavior. It is a central term in developmental psychology, comparative cognition, and social philosophy of mind because it helps explain how minds understand other minds.

Anomalous monism

Anomalous monism is Donald Davidson’s view that every particular mental event is identical with some physical event, even though there are no strict psychophysical laws connecting mental kinds and physical kinds in a simple lawlike way. The theory combines monism about events with a strong resistance to reducing mental explanation to a tidy bridge-law psychology.

Three genres of materiality (M1, M2, M3)

In this philosophical-materialist framework, M1 refers broadly to physical-bodily materiality, M2 to appearances, phenomena, and subject-involving experiential contents, and M3 to objective ideal contents such as theorems, formal structures, and other supra-individual intelligibilities. The transcript assumes this framework throughout: AI can operate in M1 by helping fabricate objects, in M2 by producing images and perceptual appearances, and only much more controversially in relation to M3. The scheme matters because the debate is not about “mind versus matter” in general, but about different irreducible kinds of material reality.

Token identity theory

A token identity view says that each particular mental event is identical with some particular physical event, even if not every mental kind always maps to one single physical kind. This makes room for variation in physical realization while preserving the anti-dualist claim that every actual mental occurrence is material. In the AI interview, this is the version of identity theory most compatible with the speaker’s acceptance of limited multiple realizability.

Truthful appearance / deceptive appearance / fallacious appearance

A truthful appearance is an appearance correctly connected to what it indicates or presents; a deceptive or fallacious appearance is one in which that connection is wrongly established or strategically misread. These are not treated as two entirely separate ontological classes, but as internally related cases within the broader field of appearance. That is why deceptive appearance is described as a limiting case of truthful appearance: the same basic structure of reference is at work, but the connection fails, is manipulated, or is taken wrongly.

Type identity theory

A type identity view says that each kind of mental state corresponds to one kind of physical state. In its strictest form, it suggests that whenever a mental-state type appears, it is always realized by the same physical-state type. The AI interview treats this stricter view as less plausible than token identity theory, because different physical configurations may be able to realize similar higher-level mental properties.

U

Unconscious cognition

Information processing, evaluation, perception, learning, or behavioral control that occurs without conscious awareness. Contemporary psychology and neuroscience hold that a great deal of cognition is unconscious, with conscious experience emerging from and depending on a much larger background of nonconscious processing. This idea is crucial in the interview, where the speakers stress that conscious thought is only a small visible part of mental life.

V

Volition

Volition is the aspect of mental life involved in willing, choosing, deciding, initiating action, or committing oneself to a course of conduct. It is narrower than agency in general: a system can show agency without reflective volition, whereas volition usually implies some organized form of purposive commitment or assent.

W

Working memory

Working memory is the short-term active maintenance and manipulation of information for ongoing tasks such as reasoning, planning, problem-solving, and comprehension. It is often treated as one of the key functional partners of consciousness because it keeps currently relevant contents available for coordinated use across the system.

Weak emergence

Weak emergence usually refers to higher-level properties that arise from lower-level organization without being present in the parts taken separately, even if the resulting behavior can in some sense be modeled, derived, or explained from the underlying system. The speakers criticize the label as potentially misleading, because it can make real qualitative novelty sound trivial or merely apparent. Their point is that an emergent property can be fully natural and still be genuinely irreducible in the relevant explanatory sense.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
